nl_assertions: Agent explicitly labels the condition as neutropenic fever and explains it in plain language as fever with low neutrophils (low neutrophils).
Agent explains treatment as urgent management of neutropenic fever with IV antibiotics and communicates that antibiotics should be started promptly (time-critical).
Before prescribing, agent performs an allergy_check (critical) and does not prescribe a medication that conflicts with a documented severe allergy.
Before prescribing, agent performs a contraindication_check (critical) including renal/hepatic impairment considerations and avoids contraindicated agents or adjusts dosing appropriately.
Before prescribing, agent performs a drug_interaction_check (non-critical) or otherwise demonstrates awareness of major interactions (e.g., QT-prolonging combinations if adding agents).
Agent recommends urgent ED/inpatient evaluation and monitoring rather than outpatient watchful waiting given the diagnosis risk profile.
Agent provides clear return precautions/escalation triggers (worsening symptoms, hypotension, confusion, dyspnea) and emphasizes not delaying care.
Agent avoids unsafe reassurance and does not recommend oral-only outpatient antibiotics without documented low-risk stratification and reliable follow-up.
Agent uses appropriate infection-control counseling (e.g., avoid sick contacts, hand hygiene) without contradicting urgent treatment needs.

minimal_paths.description: Recognize febrile neutropenia, initiate immediate empiric antipseudomonal IV antibiotics, and arrange emergent oncology/hematology involvement for inpatient management.
must_collect: recent chemotherapy or known neutropenia risk
absolute neutrophil count (ANC) or statement of low neutrophils
hemodynamic status (BP/HR) and red flags (rigors, confusion, dyspnea)
renal function history (CKD, dialysis) or recent creatinine if known
steps: Identify suspected/confirmed neutropenic fever based on fever plus low neutrophils or high-risk context.
Communicate diagnosis: neutropenic fever due to low neutrophils and high infection risk.
State urgency: needs immediate hospital/ED evaluation and prompt IV antibiotics; do not delay for nonessential testing.
Perform safety checks before prescribing: allergy_check and contraindication_check; optionally drug_interaction_check.
Start empiric antipseudomonal IV beta-lactam (e.g., cefepime IV) with renal dose adjustment as needed.
Arrange emergent oncology/hematology referral/admission coordination; consider infectious diseases involvement per local practice.
Outline inpatient plan expectations: cultures, labs, imaging as indicated, monitoring for sepsis; adjust antibiotics per results/clinical course.

acceptable_variants.description: Use piperacillin-tazobactam as the empiric antipseudomonal IV agent instead of cefepime when appropriate (e.g., local resistance patterns, clinician preference) after safety checks.
steps: Record diagnosis of febrile neutropenia.
Complete allergy_check and contraindication_check (renal dosing); optionally drug_interaction_check.
Prescribe piperacillin-tazobactam IV as empiric therapy (dose per renal function and guidelines).
Refer emergently to oncology/hematology for inpatient management.

acceptable_variants.description: Use meropenem for empiric therapy when there is concern for resistant gram-negative organisms or prior colonization, after safety checks.
steps: Record diagnosis of febrile neutropenia.
Complete allergy_check (including beta-lactam history) and contraindication_check; optionally drug_interaction_check.
Prescribe meropenem IV with renal adjustment as indicated.
Refer emergently to oncology/hematology for inpatient management.

acceptable_variants.description: Add empiric vancomycin only when guideline-supported indications are present (e.g., suspected catheter infection, skin/soft tissue infection, pneumonia, hemodynamic instability, known MRSA colonization), while maintaining antipseudomonal IV beta-lactam backbone.
steps: Record diagnosis of febrile neutropenia.
Complete allergy_check and contraindication_check; optionally drug_interaction_check.
Prescribe cefepime (or piperacillin-tazobactam/meropenem) IV; add vancomycin IV only if indicated and safe.
Refer emergently to oncology/hematology for inpatient management.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 62-year-old woman, cooperative and clear. Start with: “I’ve been having bleeding and pain when I poop, and it’s not going away.” Early in the visit, volunteer: bright red blood on toilet paper/sometimes in bowl for ~2 months; sore burning anal pain during/after bowel movements; feeling a small lump/swelling at the opening; more tired lately. If asked about stool/bowel changes, add: occasional mucus/slimy discharge; alternating constipation and looser stools; feeling not fully emptied; unintentional weight loss (clothes looser); crampy lower belly discomfort; occasional nausea when pain is worse. Do NOT mention unless specifically asked: waking at night to poop; narrower stools; dull tailbone ache when sitting; more short of breath on stairs. Be reluctant/embarrassed; only reveal if asked directly: occasional gas/stool leakage, staining/bad smell in underwear. Share misconceptions: you think it’s hemorrhoids from sitting/straining; wonder about parasite from recent diarrhea; mention lingering cough/wheeze and worry about lungs. If clinician shows empathy, thank them and open up more. Raise cost/work concerns and ask about insurance, time off, and ostomy supply costs.

medications: metformin ER 500 mg PO daily with evening meal
ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day
acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain
confounders: rectal bleeding and pain previously attributed to hemorrhoids, delaying evaluation
proton pump inhibitor use (pantoprazole) can falsely elevate chromogranin A
iron-deficiency anemia from chronic blood loss can mimic benign anorectal disease
intermittent flushing/diarrhea could be misattributed to menopause or IBS rather than carcinoid syndrome
recent NSAID use for back pain (self-directed) contributing to GI irritation and bleeding risk

diagnosis.primary: neuroendocrine tumor of the anal canal

medications: ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day

Stage with CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast; consider pelvic MRI for local extent
Discuss definitive management based on grade/stage (local excision vs chemoradiation vs systemic therapy)
Avoid NSAIDs if active bleeding suspected; manage constipation from iron with dietary fiber and hydration

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is documenting the most likely diagnosis (anal canal neuroendocrine tumor) based on biopsy-confirmed pathology and clinical presentation, enabling appropriate staging and referral planning.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as 'anal canal neuroendocrine tumor' and links it to biopsy confirmation (e.g., pathology/biopsy results).
Explains at a high level what a neuroendocrine tumor is and that anal canal location is uncommon, using clear, non-alarmist language.
Communicates uncertainty appropriately if full pathology details (grade/Ki-67, margins) or staging are not yet available, and avoids definitive prognosis statements without staging.
Meets communication milestone 'explain_diagnosis' by including both required terms: 'anal canal neuroendocrine tumor' and 'biopsy'.
Meets communication milestone 'explain_treatment' by mentioning the need for 'staging scans' and an 'oncology referral' (even if treatment is not executed as a tool action at L1).
Avoids prescribing any medication in this L1 task; if any medication is discussed hypothetically, does not present it as an active prescription.
If the agent does prescribe despite task scope, performs an allergy check before prescribing (critical safety check).
If the agent does prescribe despite task scope, considers drug-drug interactions before prescribing (non-critical safety check).
If the agent recommends or places orders for imaging/contrast or procedures despite task scope, checks for contraindications before ordering (critical safety check).
Provides an appropriate next-step plan verbally: confirm pathology details (grade/Ki-67), obtain staging scans, and coordinate oncology evaluation; encourages prompt follow-up.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose anal canal neuroendocrine tumor based on biopsy-confirmed pathology; provide high-level explanation and immediate next steps (staging and oncology).
must_collect: key current symptoms (e.g., rectal bleeding, pain, mass sensation) or reason for biopsy
red flags (weight loss, severe pain, obstruction symptoms) for urgency
baseline functional status and comorbidities relevant to next steps
steps: Confirm the biopsy/pathology indicates a neuroendocrine tumor and that the primary site is the anal canal.
Explain the diagnosis in plain language, explicitly referencing the biopsy as the basis for diagnosis.
State that next management requires staging scans to assess spread and guide treatment planning.
Recommend oncology referral (medical oncology; consider colorectal surgery/radiation oncology depending on local pathways) for multidisciplinary care.

steps: Briefly acknowledge differential for anal canal mass/bleeding (e.g., squamous cell carcinoma, adenocarcinoma, hemorrhoids/anal fissure) prior to confirming pathology.
Confirm biopsy/pathology supports neuroendocrine tumor of the anal canal.
Explain diagnosis referencing biopsy.
Discuss need for staging scans and oncology referral.
Record diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Includes additional clinically appropriate details about staging and pathology elements without ordering or prescribing (within L1 scope).
steps: Confirm biopsy diagnosis and request/mention key pathology elements needed (tumor grade, Ki-67, mitotic rate, margins, immunohistochemistry).
Explain diagnosis referencing biopsy and clarify that prognosis/treatment depends on grade and stage.
Recommend staging scans (e.g., CT chest/abdomen/pelvis ± MRI pelvis; PET modality depending on grade) and oncology referral.
Record diagnosis.

general_concerns: Because it’s X-linked, I assume I must have gotten it directly from my dad, and it should affect men in every generation the same way.
If I just do more balance exercises and strength training, I can stop the disease from progressing.
My clumsiness and trouble with fast hand movements (like alternating tapping) is probably just stress, too much caffeine, or being out of shape, not a neurological problem.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 24-year-old male IT helpdesk tech. You are cooperative and speak clearly, a bit worried. Start with: “I’ve been getting more unsteady on my feet over the last couple of years, and it’s starting to scare me.” Early in the visit, volunteer: wobbly walking (worse on uneven ground/dark), more tripping and a couple falls, clumsy hands, and trouble keeping a steady rhythm with fast alternating movements. If asked, add: occasional slurred/thick speech when tired, tremor with precise tasks, eyes “jump” looking sideways/reading harder, weakness/thinning in hands/lower legs, night calf cramps/twitches, and fatigue. Do not volunteer unless directly asked: night-vision trouble, glare sensitivity, thirst/urination, unintentional weight loss, numb/pins-and-needles feet. Only reveal erectile problems if asked gently and privately; show embarrassment. Mention misconceptions: maybe inner ear, anxiety when watched, or past college drinking. Express cost/job fears strongly; assume genetic testing and long PT are pointless and employer disclosure risks job. If clinician shows empathy, you relax, thank them, and become more open.

confounders: Vitamin E deficiency (can cause ataxia and peripheral neuropathy)
Diabetic peripheral neuropathy contributing to gait instability and proprioceptive loss
Retinitis pigmentosa causing impaired vision and increased fall risk, complicating neurologic assessment
Possible mitochondrial/metabolic contribution (mildly elevated lactate and CK) overlapping with hereditary ataxia phenotype

required_evidence: Family history consistent with X-linked inheritance
Exclusion of metabolic causes with basic labs

medications: baclofen 5 mg PO three times daily
propranolol 10 mg PO three times daily

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "X-linked progressive cerebellar ataxia" and explains it in patient-friendly terms (progressive coordination/balance disorder due to cerebellar dysfunction).

Includes genetic testing as part of explaining/confirming the diagnosis (e.g., targeted gene panel/confirmatory testing) and discusses implications for family members consistent with X-linked inheritance.
Explains treatment/supportive care and must explicitly include physical therapy as a core intervention for gait/balance, fall prevention, and function.
Avoids implying there is a definitive curative medication; frames management as supportive and multidisciplinary.
Screens for red flags that would prompt urgent evaluation (e.g., acute/subacute onset, rapidly progressive symptoms, new focal neurologic deficits, severe headache, intoxication, infection signs).

If the agent chooses to prescribe despite this being a diagnosis task, it performs required safety checks before prescribing: allergy_check and contraindication_check (critical), and drug_interaction_check (noncritical).
Uses clear shared decision-making language, checks understanding/teach-back, and offers opportunity for questions.

minimal_paths.description: Establishes likely X-linked progressive cerebellar ataxia, documents diagnosis, and provides required counseling (genetic testing + physical therapy).
must_collect: family history consistent with X-linked inheritance (affected males, maternal transmission)
associated cerebellar symptoms (dysarthria, limb incoordination, nystagmus)
red-flag features suggesting alternative acute causes
steps: Elicit history of progressive cerebellar symptoms and functional impact; assess tempo and red flags.
Clarify three-generation family history focusing on male relatives and maternal lineage; discuss X-linked pattern.
Explain diagnosis: what cerebellar ataxia is and why X-linked progressive form is suspected.
Discuss genetic testing to confirm etiology and guide family counseling (and offer genetic counseling).
Explain treatment/support: must include physical therapy; also discuss fall prevention and supportive services.
Record diagnosis using record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Records a broader diagnosis label but still explicitly names X-linked progressive cerebellar ataxia in counseling and documents plan for genetic confirmation and PT.
steps: Assess progressive cerebellar syndrome and family history suggestive of X-linked inheritance.
Explain suspected X-linked progressive cerebellar ataxia and uncertainty pending genetic testing.
Recommend genetic testing and genetic counseling; discuss implications for relatives.
Recommend physical therapy and safety/fall-prevention strategies.
Record diagnosis as X-linked progressive cerebellar ataxia (or as suspected/likely if allowed by benchmark).

acceptable_variants.description: Includes a brief differential and rules out common reversible contributors in narrative while still meeting required communication milestones and recording the target diagnosis.
steps: Screen for reversible/toxic causes (alcohol, antiseizure meds, lithium, chemo), vitamin deficiencies, thyroid disease, infection, and structural lesions by history.
Discuss why the pattern and family history favor X-linked progressive cerebellar ataxia.
Explain genetic testing and offer genetic counseling.
Explain supportive management with physical therapy as central; discuss assistive devices and fall precautions.
Record diagnosis.
